english_source_text: A human embryo at the blastocyst stage. Cloned embryos were used as a source of stem cells, which can produce new heart muscles, bones, brain tissue, or any other type of body cell. The study, published in the specialized journal Cell, used methods similar to those that led to the creation of the sheep Dolly in the UK. However, researchers say that other sources of stem cells could be easier, cheaper, and less controversial. Stem cells are one of the great hopes for medicine. The ability to create new tissue could facilitate healing from damage caused by a heart attack or repair a severed spinal cord. Tests are already underway using stem cells from donated embryos to restore sight to patients. However, these donated cells do not match the patient, so they would be rejected by their body. Cloning avoids this problem. The technique used - somatic cell nuclear transfer - has been well known since the sheep Dolly became the first cloned mammal in 1996. The birth of Dolly in 1996 was considered a significant scientific breakthrough. Cells were obtained from the skin of an adult and the genetic information was placed inside a...
english_translation: The first human cloning technique may be one of the great hopes for medicine.

english_source_text: It is believed that the solstices have been celebrated at Stonehenge for thousands of years. The start of the longest day of the year in that part of the planet brought together about 10,000 people at the Neolithic monument, according to Wiltshire Police, the county in the UK where it is located. "There was a lovely and friendly atmosphere, the sun was shining, and the dawn was welcomed with shouts of joy," said Kate Logan from the English Heritage organization. Many who came to greet the solstice at Stonehenge believe that the Neolithic structure is a very spiritual place. Some visitors take advantage of the solstice at Stonehenge to reflect or recharge. The stones of the circle align with the sunrise on the summer solstice and with the sunset on the winter solstice. On the summer solstice, the sun rises behind the Heel Stone, the ancient entrance to the site, and the rays of sunlight are channeled into the center of the structure. The hill known as Glastonbury Tor in Somerset, in southern England, also attracted crowds wanting to celebrate the first day of summer. Many modern "druids" (who follow ancient Celtic religious beliefs) also go to await the summer solstice at Stonehenge.
english_translation: The summer solstice is celebrated at Stonehenge, in southern England.

english_source_text: Evo Morales is recovering after tearing the anterior cruciate ligament in his left knee. The president of Bolivia was diagnosed with the nightmare of every football player: a torn anterior cruciate ligament in the knee. He underwent surgery last week and starting this Monday, he begins a long rehabilitation process that will require him to use crutches for four weeks and undergo a lengthy physical therapy period of over eight months. But this is not the worst for Morales. According to the diagnosis from the Bolivian doctors who treated him, he will not be able to play football for at least a year. Despite the bleak outlook, Evo has no doubts that sooner or later he will be scoring goals on one of the many fields he inaugurated in Bolivia during his more than 10 years in office. 'For now, I am focused on recovering quickly. Soon we will be playing, that is true, for me, sports continue to be the best fun,' said an optimistic Morales on Sunday, just minutes before leaving the private clinic where he was treated. In politics, he is leftist, but on the field, Morales kicks with his right. That statement sounds more like that of a professional football player than a head of state, but it must be considered that for Morales, football is a very important part of his personal history. Perhaps that is why, on the verge of turning 57...
english_translation: The president of Bolivia, Evo Morales, is recovering after tearing the anterior cruciate ligament in his left knee. He was diagnosed with the nightmare of every football player: a torn anterior cruciate ligament in the knee.

english_source_text: The reddish powder covering the remains of the Red Queen is known as cinnabar, a toxic mineral composed of mercury and sulfur, used to preserve human remains. On the lid of the sarcophagus, they found a hole, through which archaeologist Arnulfo González took a look and shouted: "It's full of jade! It's the hallucination, of the hallucination, of the hallucination!" It was the morning of June 1, 1994. In addition to jade, the bones, stones, and seashells inside were covered by a reddish powder - cinnabar, a mineral composed of mercury and sulfur, used to preserve human remains - which led to the woman buried there 1,346 years ago, on November 13, 672 AD, being called "the Red Queen." Her remains have traveled through New York and Los Angeles, in the U.S.; Palenque, in Mexico; and even Ontario, in Canada. But now, 24 years after her discovery, the burial goods of this Maya queen are being exhibited for the first time at the Museum of the Templo Mayor in Mexico City, where it will be open until September 9. But who was this woman and how did science come to know her identity? What does she tell us about the Maya world? The burial goods of the Red Queen consist of seven pieces —mask, diadem, necklace, pectoral, headdress, shell, and figurine—. Tz'ak-b'u Ajaw, the Red Queen. The analysis of her remains led to...
english_translation: The Red Queen, the aristocrat who died in Mexico, was discovered in a Mexican National Anthropology Museum.

english_source_text: Toyota has benefited from sales in the markets of Japan and the United States. The automotive giant predicts a net profit of $9.7 billion for the financial year ending March 31, 2013, thanks to a strong recovery in sales in Japan and the U.S. Toyota based its profit projection after recording a 221% increase in the quarter from July to September compared to the same period last year. "During the first six months of this fiscal year, we have seen a significant increase in production across all regions compared to the same period last year, when we suffered a shortage of parts supply," said Satoshi Ozawa, executive vice president of Toyota. However, the Japanese firm showed caution as its sales could be affected by the ongoing territorial dispute between Japan and China and the severe crisis affecting several countries in Europe.
english_translation: Toyota announced a significant increase in its profits for the upcoming year.

english_source_text: The number of people using bicycles as a means of transportation is increasing every year in Europe, the United States, and Latin America. In the United States, for example, the number of people commuting to work by bicycle increased by 60% in the last decade, according to a report published by the census office last June. The 786,000 people represent only 0.6% compared to 2.9% in England and Wales. Public bicycle systems are expanding in Latin American cities. In Latin America, public bicycle systems like EcoBici in Mexico City or exclusive bike lanes like those in Bogotá continue to emerge, a trend that is repeating in other cities across the continent. The increase in 'beginners' has also led to more advice from 'veterans' who seek to address the questions that arise among those starting to cycle. In BBC Mundo, we clarify five of the main concerns that are repeated in discussion forums among cycling enthusiasts. Does it help with weight loss? Cycling is an aerobic activity, which is the type of exercise recommended for losing weight and body fat. Weight loss varies according to the intensity applied while cycling. The point is that weight loss...
english_translation: The use of bicycles as a means of transportation is growing in Europe, the United States, and Latin America.

english_source_text: Despite centuries of domestication, European strawberries remained a small delicacy in 1711. However, in a region between the Andes and the Pacific, indigenous people had been cultivating and enhancing them for nearly a thousand years. Julius de Berry from Antwerp had gifted a bowl full of ripe forest strawberries; as a reward, the king named the family 'Fraise' (strawberry in French). 800 years later, another king of France, Louis XIV, was worried because his country was not faring well in the War of Spanish Succession and feared that a peace treaty would leave the court of Versailles without access to the riches of America. In 1711, he decided to send a spy to the south of the New World to gather information. The chosen one turned out to be none other than Amedée François Frézier. The surname had changed slightly, as the family had settled in Scotland in the Middle Ages, as part of the entourage of the French ambassador, and there it became Frazer; upon returning to France, it became Frézier, but Amedée was a descendant of Julius, who had gifted strawberries to Charles 'the Simple'. In one of those curious coincidences of history, his journey to South America would forever change that delicacy that so pleased the monarch.
english_translation: An Indonesian domestic worker who was abused by her employer in Saudi Arabia will receive a record compensation of $80,000.

english_source_text: Hundreds of migrants attempted to enter at once through the border between Greece and Macedonia. Most of the migrants come from the Middle East in an effort to settle in northern EU countries, fleeing violence, especially from the war in Syria and northern Iraq. The European Union urged both Greece and Macedonia to address the 'deteriorating situation.' About 44,000 people have arrived in the EU through Macedonia in the last two months, where they faced little resistance at the border. Security forces had to use tear gas to disperse the migrants. This has led authorities to erect barbed wire fences to prevent crossings. BBC correspondent James Reynolds reported that hundreds of migrants clashed with security forces at the border just this Friday afternoon. The humanitarian group Doctors Without Borders indicated that they have treated several people with injuries from stun grenades fired by Macedonian soldiers in the border town of Edomeni. Meanwhile, Amnesty International criticized Macedonian authorities for 'responding as if they were dealing with protests instead of a humanitarian crisis.'
english_translation: The latest wave of migrants at the border between Greece and Macedonia has been labeled as 'the majority of illegal migrants' in the country.

english_source_text: Photos of 83-year-old Wolfram Gottschalk and his 81-year-old wife Anita have been shared many times online, accompanied by stories from their grandchildren. Granddaughter Ashley Baryik announced that her grandparents were sent to different care homes due to a lack of space at the nursing home in British Columbia. In a Facebook post on Tuesday, Baryik described the photo as 'the saddest picture I've ever taken.' The image has been shared over 6,000 times. Baryik also revealed that the couple was separated when Wolfram Gottschalk was hospitalized due to a heart condition. Following his hospitalization, the grandmother applied to move to a care home that required closer care. However, when the grandfather was discharged from the hospital, he could not move into the home where his wife lived as planned. The couple, who have been living apart for 8 months, is still waiting for a space to open up in the home where Anita Gottshalk resides. Speaking to the BBC, Baryik said the situation has been quite challenging for her grandparents.
english_translation: A couple married for 62 years in Canada had to separate due to a lack of space in the nursing home. Wolfram and Anita Gottschalk have been living apart for 8 months.

english_source_text: In a statement from the foundation, it was reported that the Board of Trustees approved this proposal with the thought that the recent intensification of unfounded claims and disproportionate speculations in the media about the foundation made it impossible to continue its activities. Ali Hakan Altınay, the Chairman of the Open Society Foundation's Management and Advisory Board, was detained during an operation held recently and was later released. The investigation in which Altınay was detained is linked to Osman Kavala, the Chairman of Anadolu Kültür, a human rights activist and businessman, who is still in custody. He is accused of using the Open Society Foundation and Anadolu Kültür A.Ş. to finance and organize events aimed at spreading the Gezi Park protests that began on May 27, 2013, across Turkey, creating an environment of chaos and disorder, and thereby attempting to overthrow the government of the Republic of Turkey or partially or completely hinder its functions through the use of force and violence. The Open Society Foundations were established in 1984 by Hungarian-American businessman George Soros and rapidly expanded by opening offices in former Communist countries after the end of the Cold War. It is claimed on their website that Soros's work has contributed to the emergence of democratic governments in many countries within the former Soviet empire.
english_translation: The Open Society Foundation has decided to cease its activities starting this week.

english_source_text: Yenigün was first suspended from his school and then dismissed after signing the Academics for Peace declaration. He was in the United States, where he had been for a long time, giving conferences during the July 15 coup attempt. During this period, when passports were canceled and exits from the country were highly controlled, he abandoned his short visit to Turkey and headed to the university in Germany that had accepted him. Yenigün, who received a scholarship in Islamic Studies at the Free University of Berlin, is now drawing attention to a changing situation. Years ago, he studied in Turkey and later completed his doctorate in the U.S. before returning to the country. He states that Turkey used to have an allure for those studying abroad to return, but this has now changed: 'Generally, those who go for a doctorate to the U.S. from other countries tend to stay there. Turkey had a unique situation, particularly for academics, which was to return. No matter what, Turkey was a country that could attract its citizens back. This situation has changed.' After the coup attempt, 15 universities were closed by decrees. The highest number of applications to the Scholar Rescue Fund, established by the International Institute of Education (IIE), came from Turkey, as noted by Sarah Willcox, the director of the fund, who participated in an event in September.
english_translation: "I thought I was suspended from the university for signing the Academics for Peace declaration, but here we are a year later, and those who went abroad have started to return. We are among those who returned a year later. This situation has now left an uncertainty in the international community regarding Turkey," says Prof. Dr. Melih Yenigün.

english_source_text: In recent weeks, rebels have attacked multiple times to seize the airport. The city of Donetsk is under the control of the rebels. However, government forces maintain control of the airport located outside the city in the northwest. Capturing the airport is significant for the rebels as they believe it will allow them to supply their needs. Vladislav Selezniyov from the Ukrainian administration stated in an interview with Kanal 5 TV that the army repelled four attacks on the airport on Wednesday evening. Selezniyov mentioned that a T-64 tank was destroyed and seven rebels were killed. Conflicting reports are emerging about who controls the airport. A reporter from the Associated Press in Donetsk indicated signs that the army has lost control of the airport. One of the rebel leaders, Aleksandr Zakharchenko, claims that 90% of the airport is under their control. Ukrainian officials insist that the airport remains under their control. Since April, more than 3,500 people have died in the ongoing conflict between the government and pro-Russian separatists. The ceasefire signed between the parties on September 5 is frequently violated. The World Bank has announced that Ukraine's economy is expected to shrink by 8% this year.
english_translation: Conflicts continue between pro-Russian separatists and government forces in Ukraine. In recent weeks, rebels have attacked multiple times to seize the airport. The city of Donetsk is under the control of the rebels.

english_source_text: Research shows that cats are much more independent than dogs. It is difficult to get cats to do something; they do what they want. Those who keep cats know this. But why do cats not like to cooperate with each other or with humans? Or conversely, why do many other animals, whether domestic or wild, prefer to live in groups? The advantages of living in a group are quite common in nature. Birds, antelopes, and fish live in groups. Predators often hunt in groups as well. Lions, which belong to the cat family, also live in groups. Being in a group means being safer. A predator will only hunt one of them, and if there are a hundred animals in the group, the chance of being hunted drops to one percent. When alone, that chance is one hundred percent. Some animals live in groups for their benefits. It means that the animals in the group have many eyes. The likelihood of spotting an approaching predator increases, allowing for a better chance to escape. This cooperation in vigilance also allows individual animals to spend more time finding food. However, the issue is not just avoiding being prey. Animals that move in groups do not need to search for mates to breed. When offspring are born, the responsibility of protecting and feeding them belongs to almost the entire group. In many bird species, the young are raised until they are ready to mate.
english_translation: Why don’t cats live in groups? Research shows that cats are much more independent than dogs. It is difficult to get cats to do something; they do what they want. Those who keep cats know this. But why do cats not like to cooperate with each other or with humans?

english_source_text: In a study conducted by scientists from Imperial College London, 19 patients were given a single dose of a psychedelic substance called psilocybin. More than half of the patients reported not feeling depressed after taking the substance, and changes in brain function were observed. The positive effects of psilocybin on individuals with depression lasted for about 5 weeks. However, the research team warned that those suffering from depression should not attempt this method on their own. Previous studies had also shown that psilocybin could help individuals break free from the cycle of depressive symptoms by 'lubricating the mind.' The research team used fMRI (functional magnetic resonance imaging) to scan the patients' brains before and after the administration of psilocybin. The results, published in the journal Scientific Reports, revealed that psilocybin affected two key areas of the brain. Its structure resembles that of the 'happiness hormone' serotonin. The substance reduces the activity of the amygdala, a region that plays a significant role in processing emotions such as fear and anxiety. As the functions in this area decrease, so do the symptoms of depression. Additionally, psilocybin makes the brain's default-mode network, which is active when not focused on a specific task and consists of communication between different regions, more stable. The structure of psilocybin...
english_translation: Psychedelic substances, or 'magic mushrooms,' known to protect the mind from severe emotional stress, can be used in the treatment of depression.

english_source_text: The symbolic clock is two minutes away from 'striking midnight.' The 'Doomsday Clock' is updated by scientists who are members of the Bulletin of the Atomic Scientists (BAS). Updated since 1947, the clock serves as a metaphor for how close humanity is to destroying the world. According to scientists, the world has become an increasingly 'dangerous' place, and it has never been this close to 'doomsday' since 1953. In 1953, after both the US and Russia tested hydrogen bombs, the clock was moved forward again, marking two minutes to midnight. Last year, it was also moved forward by 30 seconds. What is the reason for this decision? The committee announcing the decision in Washington, D.C., stated that making this decision was not easy and that there is no single reason behind it. BAS President Rachel Bronson said that this year's discussions centered around nuclear issues. North Korea's nuclear tests, rising tensions in Korea, and the verbal war between the US and North Korea have been the most concerning topics for scientists. BAS also noted that the US's strategy to increase nuclear spending is alarming. The rising tensions between Russia and the West were also factors influencing the decision. Scientists say that when the clock strikes midnight, it will signal the end of the world due to global threats like climate change and the struggles of international institutions fighting against them.
english_translation: The committee announcing the decision in Washington, D.C., stated that making this decision was not easy and that there is no single reason behind it. BAS President Rachel Bronson said that this year's discussions centered around nuclear issues.

english_source_text: According to the report, India is one of the countries with the highest rates of child marriage. The organization's research indicates that in some countries, including Afghanistan, Yemen, India, and Somalia, girls as young as 10 are forced into marriage. Save the Children noted that early marriage can disadvantage girls in every aspect of their lives. Factors driving girls into early marriage include conflict, poverty, and humanitarian crises. On International Day of the Girl Child, child brides are a global issue: there are 700 million child brides. A girl is married every 7 seconds. Turkey ranks 55th among 144 countries. Niger is the country most affected by the child bride issue. Helle Thorning-Schmidt, Chair of Save the Children, stated, "Child marriages deprive girls of their fundamental rights to learn, develop, and be children. Early married girls cannot go to school and are more likely to face domestic violence, abuse, and rape. They become pregnant and are exposed to sexually transmitted diseases, including HIV." In the report titled "Every Last Girl," countries are ranked based on school enrollment, child marriage, pregnancy, maternal deaths, and the number of women in parliament. Turkey ranks 55th among 144 countries. At the bottom of the ranking are Chad, Niger, the Central African Republic, Mali, and Somalia.
english_translation: On International Day of the Girl Child, one of the countries with the highest number of child brides.

english_source_text: Saigon Giai Phong online reported that a document from the Department of Journalism dated June 4, 2008 concluded that the website vietbao.vn "has been and is providing information on the Internet without permission from the competent authority." Reports indicate that one of the reasons vietbao.vn faced scrutiny was that it had taken news from other newspapers without permission. Another significant reason, according to Lao Dong online, is that this website "reposted several articles from blogs on sensitive and complex issues that do not align with the policies and foreign affairs of the Vietnamese government." Currently, Vietnam does not have specific regulations for managing blogs, even though this topic has been discussed since August 2007. In an interview with the BBC late last year, the head of the Department of Journalism, Hoang Huu Luong, stated that blogging is not prohibited. However, Vu Xuan Thanh, Chief Inspector of the Ministry of Culture and Information, mentioned that the government could rely on Decree 55/CP (on the management, provision, and use of Internet services) to address the dissemination of content and images that "go against moral standards and information against the Party and the State" through blogs. It is known that in 2007 and 2008, the Department of Journalism had sent documents to the Ministry's Inspectorate, the police, and the Vietnam Internet Center, requesting relevant agencies to check activities...
english_translation: The vietbao.vn website has been concluded by the Department of Journalism to have violated press laws.

english_source_text: Many injured people had to stand outside the burning factory waiting for rescue teams. Images shared on social media show several burn victims waiting for assistance outside the factory complex, where black smoke is rising. Rescue efforts are underway as authorities investigate the cause of the explosion. China's CCTV reported that this factory belongs to Zhongrong Plating, a company specializing in manufacturing auto parts with about 450 workers. The company's clients include the U.S. corporation General Motors, CCTV added. Chinese state media reported that the incident occurred inside a workshop polishing wheel rims. About 200 workers were said to be working at the factory at the time of the explosion. More than 200 workers were reportedly present at the factory when the explosion occurred.
english_translation: A factory in southern China caught fire, where injured people were involved in an explosion.

english_source_text: This time, Biden enters the race as the presumptive front-runner, the most famous and favored among Democrats aspiring to be president. The 77-year-old faced early setbacks in the party meeting in Iowa and the New Hampshire primary but took control of the race with a series of victories on Super Tuesday. Although Obama called him 'the best vice president America has ever had,' Biden's record from four decades in public office has been attacked. So who is Joe Biden, and what are his main policies? A familiar political face, Biden was the Democratic candidate in 2008 before dropping out and joining Barack Obama's ticket. Eight years in the White House during Obama's presidency—where he frequently appeared alongside the president—allowed Biden to claim some credit for much of Obama's legacy, including the passage of the Affordable Care Act (Obamacare), the economic stimulus package, and financial reform. Biden's frequent references to his close relationship with Obama—whom he often calls 'brother'—may have helped him gain long-term support from African American voters. As a long-time insider in Washington, Biden has solid foreign policy information and helped balance Obama's political inexperience at the time. The nickname 'Middle... [repetition continues]
english_translation: Joe Biden is entering the presidential race for the third time in less than two years.

english_source_text: General Chulanont was sworn in during a brief ceremony in the capital Bangkok at the end of Sunday, just a few hours after the announcement was made. He stated that his government would focus on ensuring the 'happiness of the people' over economic development. The swearing-in ceremony was conducted by the leader of the coup, General Sonthi Boonyaratglin. Prime Minister Chulanont is expected to announce new internal personnel this week. Mr. Surayud Chulanont, 62 years old, is a former military officer and one of the few senior figures in Thailand respected by both the military and civilian sectors. With his selection as the new Prime Minister, the Thai military hopes to alleviate international community concerns. The military's interim constitution was also announced on Thai television, stating that it had been approved by King Bhumibol. According to this interim constitution, the military government is expected to retain significant power until the next elections in Thailand, which are promised to be held in October 2007. Military officers took power in Thailand after a bloodless coup on September 19. The coup leader, General Sonthi Boonyaratglin, was quoted by Reuters...
english_translation: General Chulanont was sworn in during a brief ceremony in the capital Bangkok at the end of Sunday, just a few hours after the announcement was made.

english_source_text: Carwyn Jones will meet with politicians and business leaders, starting in Washington DC on Monday. He stated that trade and investment from North America will become increasingly important after Brexit. Jones, who campaigned against leaving the EU, mentioned that there are 'exciting opportunities' ahead. He will also visit Canada and New York, where he will speak at a UN meeting on women's rights and meet Hillary Clinton. This trip includes events themed around St. David's Day to promote Wales as a destination for businesses and tourists. North America is Wales' largest export market after Europe. Official figures show that export value has decreased since 2012 but rose to nearly £2.5 billion in 2016. The Welsh government reported that 270 American companies employ nearly 50,000 staff here. New York is one of the stops on Carwyn Jones' North America trip. Before landing in Washington, Jones said, 'The US is Wales' most important business partner, and I want to build a strong trade relationship between our two countries as we leave the EU.' 'While in the US, I want to better understand the US position on future trade agreements with the UK and advocate for the development of a free trade agreement between our two countries. The trade opportunities with North America are exciting and lie ahead, and in discussions with... [repetition continues]
english_translation: Wales' Trade Minister Carwyn Jones said he wants to persuade the US to support a free trade agreement after the UK leaves the EU.

english_source_text: The Bush administration condemned the use of military force to change governments. The United States stated that a coup is an improper act. Washington warned Thailand that the military coup would require the U.S. to reassess aspects of its diplomatic relations under U.S. law. On Thursday, U.S. State Department spokesman Sean McCormack announced that the U.S. would suspend approximately $24 million in aid. This aid was intended for military training activities, peacekeeping operations, arms purchases, and counter-terrorism efforts. However, Washington stated that other funding for humanitarian programs would continue. The U.S. State Department also indicated that military support would be resumed once Thailand returned to a democratic regime. Thailand has proven to be a loyal ally in the U.S. war on terror. Three years ago, a senior figure linked to al-Qaeda was captured and handed over to the U.S. Of the total aid cut, $16 million was allocated for counter-terrorism efforts. The U.S. called the recent military coup a setback for Thailand and urged the country to return to democracy as soon as possible.
english_translation: In a military coup, the Thai army overthrew the civilian government of Prime Minister Thaksin Shinawatra.

english_source_text: Formosa's contractors stated that Vietnamese labor has not met many demands in construction. This labor figure is part of a proposal made by the management board of the economic zone in Ha Tinh province, central Vietnam, on August 22, according to VOV news. Previously, on July 29, Formosa Ha Tinh also requested the Ha Tinh provincial government to allow contractors of 28 packages to recruit 8,426 foreign workers, as reported by VOV. An article on VnExpress published on September 6 mentioned that the newly recruited labor mainly consists of 'management staff, experts, technicians, and site supervisors.' The wave of protests leading to violence in the Vung Ang industrial zone last May resulted in many foreign facilities being vandalized and thousands of Chinese workers returning home. In an interview last week with a Vietnamese newspaper, Mr. Vuong Van Tuong, Deputy Head of Formosa Ha Tinh, stated that before the violent incidents in May, Formosa Ha Tinh had 26,000 workers on site, including 5,000 Chinese. He also mentioned that the proposal to recruit over 8,400 foreign workers aims to restore production activities to 'normal.' 'In August, Formosa requested contractors to assess how many workers are needed at each stage, especially Chinese labor... The results indicate that approximately 8,400 Chinese workers are expected,' Mr. Tuong said. At the end of August, domestic newspapers reported that 30 contractors are working on the packages...
english_translation: Formosa Ha Tinh Company proposed to recruit an additional 3,000 foreign workers, primarily Chinese citizens, to support the $10 billion steel project.

english_source_text: A car bomb exploded outside Mustansiriyah University, followed by a suicide bomber targeting students fleeing the scene. In other parts of the city, at least 25 people died in car bombings and shootings. This information was released after the United Nations reported that over 34,000 Iraqis had died in violence nationwide in 2006. The UN also stated that more than 34,400 civilians had been killed in the past year. The UN's statistics are three times higher than the estimates of the Iraqi government. The latest wave of violence occurred less than a week after U.S. President George W. Bush sent an additional 20,000 troops to Iraq. Most of these soldiers are en route to Baghdad to participate in a U.S.-Iraqi military operation aimed at stabilizing security in the city. In the deadliest incident, a car bomb exploded just outside Mustansiriyah University at the end of classes. A suicide bomber then detonated among the fleeing crowd. A BBC reporter in Baghdad noted that the bombers seemed to target the main and rear gates of the building to inflict as many casualties on students as possible. Reuters quoted a university representative stating that most of the victims...
english_translation: Car bomb explosions outside a university in Baghdad, Iraq, killed at least 25 Iraqis.
